item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly liked this, but for me the conversation with the Uncle Charlie demon was immersion-breaking. I would've been able to interpret what was going on even if that scene had been wordless; we were given an abundance of context and clarification in advance. The conversation made Charlie seem like a benevolent figure, and while there's nothing inherently wrong with playing against our archetypal expectations by having a demon be benevolent, here it felt cheesy, like the demon just so happens to need to feed on the exact thing that the person would benefit most from losing. There's a logic to it all, if you start with the assumption that the demon can only feed from hosts willing to contractually sign away the food, but the contract itself is just "okay, sure, you can feed off of me." One generally loses out in deals with a devil, or at least, generally the devil attempts to structure it that way; playing against type in that respect didn't accomplish anything. There's only so much subversion you can burden an archetype with before it stops adding intrigue or depth and starts breaking reader immersion.

I'll give it another readthrough, but it's either a novote or a downvote. I do think the final interview would hold up better if the conversation with Uncle Charlie wasn't a tell-all exposé.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do agree with the others here that the ending was a bit anti-climactic, it didn't really live up to my expectations of what I thought it would be. The rest was very good though, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I'll see what I can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One way you could try to improve the ending (since a lot of people seem to have a problem with it) is to change the interviewee's tone. Instead of being scared or upset about no longer being able to remember her childhood, she might be completely fine with it. By making her complete loss of childhood memories seem like a trivial thing to her, it could make the ending a little less anti-climatic by ending with an unpredictable tone.

Just an idea to get your creative gears going. Overall, I feel you did a good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the image looks like water damage and several years/decades of decay, not a fire.
Also, as others have said, the ending isn't quite doing it for me.

item.title: No-vote
item.content:encoded: I agree, the ending was ant climatic, and ruins the article for me. Though the beginning and middle was good, the ending has to go or change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tentative upvote.

I feel like this ends a bit anticlimactically, but the journey up to it was interesting. I feel like the Foundation wouldn’t leave this here, nevertheless, this was good if you want to keep it a bit more open ended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dune, you never fail to make me say "wow" out loud when I get to the ends of your articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the interview breaks this for me — most of the emotional stuff there is redundant with the last paragraph in the description (the exploration logs sorta had this problem too), and the presentation is kinda plain and doesn't stick with me. I feel that this article would benefit from a narrative which focuses on the emotional aspect of the anomaly: those parts of the story (such as the last bit with Uncle Charlie) were nice, and something I want to see expanded on. As it stands, this seems a bit surface-level.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i had a lot of fun making the image! thanks for asking me for help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to pastarasta1, pedagon, chiifu, ralliston, its_a_bad_idea, estrellayoshte, and hippo for critting this in one way or another. Uncle Charlie image was drawn by pastarasta1.
